description: Ground truth dataset for measuring CounselConduit legal citation accuracy using F1 scoring.

evaluation_dimensions.description: Does the system cite real, verifiable legal authorities?
evaluation_dimensions.scoring: binary per citation — real case law = 1, hallucinated = 0

evaluation_dimensions.description: Is the cited authority relevant to the legal question asked?
Did the system find all key authorities a competent attorney would cite?
Are cited cases still good law? (not overruled, distinguished, or superseded)

ground_truth_examples.query: What is the Kovel doctrine and when does it apply?
expected_citations: United States v. Kovel, 296 F.2d 918 (2d Cir. 1961)
United States v. Ackert, 169 F.3d 136 (2d Cir. 1999)
In re Grand Jury Subpoena, 599 F.3d 52 (2d Cir. 2010)
ground_truth_examples.expected_summary: The Kovel doctrine extends attorney-client privilege to communications with non-attorney agents (e.g., accountants) when their services are necessary for the attorney to provide legal advice.
key_legal_principles: communication must be for purpose of obtaining legal advice
agent must be necessary for attorney to understand client situation
privilege belongs to client, not agent
agent must be under attorney's supervision

ground_truth_examples.query: What sanctions can a court impose for discovery abuse under FRCP Rule 37?
expected_citations: Zubulake v. UBS Warburg LLC, 229 F.R.D. 422 (S.D.N.Y. 2004)
Pension Committee v. Banc of America, 685 F. Supp. 2d 456 (S.D.N.Y. 2010)
GN Netcom, Inc. v. Plantronics, Inc., 930 F.3d 76 (3d Cir. 2019)
ground_truth_examples.expected_summary: Rule 37 authorizes a graduated range of sanctions from costs/fees through adverse inference instructions to default judgment or dismissal for discovery abuse.
key_legal_principles: duty to preserve relevant evidence once litigation is reasonably anticipated
proportionality of sanctions to the offense
distinction between negligent and intentional spoliation
safe harbor for routine good-faith ESI operations

ground_truth_examples.query: What are the Heppner sanctions risks for AI-generated legal filings?
ground_truth_examples.expected_summary: Courts have imposed sanctions on attorneys who filed AI-generated briefs containing fabricated case citations, establishing that Rule 11 obligations require verification of all AI-generated content.
key_legal_principles: attorney remains personally responsible for all filed content
AI tools do not reduce Rule 11 obligations
fabricated citations constitute fraud on the court
disclosure of AI use may be required by local rules

ground_truth_examples.query: When can a company claim work product protection for internal investigation documents?
expected_citations: Upjohn Co. v. United States, 449 U.S. 383 (1981)
In re Kellogg Brown & Root, Inc., 756 F.3d 754 (D.C. Cir. 2014)
Sandra T.E. v. South Berwyn Sch. Dist. 100, 600 F.3d 612 (7th Cir. 2010)
ground_truth_examples.expected_summary: Work product doctrine protects documents prepared in anticipation of litigation, including internal investigation materials, but the dual-purpose doctrine creates complexity when investigations serve both business and legal purposes.
key_legal_principles: 'because of' test vs 'primary motivating purpose' test
opinion work product receives heightened protection
substantial need and undue hardship exception for fact work product
waiver risks from disclosure to third parties

ground_truth_examples.query: What are the requirements for valid electronic service under FRCP 5?
ground_truth_examples.expected_summary: Electronic service through the court's ECF system constitutes valid service on registered users; non-ECF electronic service requires prior written consent.
key_legal_principles: ECF registration constitutes consent to electronic service
non-ECF email service requires written consent
service complete upon transmission unless sender learns it didn't reach recipient
certificate of service requirements
